questions.question_text: Describe your firm's investment strategy and philosophy.
questions.answer_text: Osprey Global Advisors pursues a global multi-strategy approach combining long/short equity, event-driven, and credit opportunities. Seeks absolute returns with low correlation to traditional benchmarks. The fund targets 12-15% net annual returns through market cycles.

questions.question_text: What is your target market segment, geography, and sector focus?
questions.answer_text: Global, all-cap. Primary focus on North America (60%) and Europe (30%), with opportunistic emerging markets exposure (10%). Multi-sector.

questions.question_text: Describe your investment process from sourcing to exit.
questions.answer_text: Multi-strategy approach driven by the CIO's market views and individual portfolio manager conviction. Each PM manages a sleeve with independent risk budgets. Centralized risk oversight.

questions.question_text: What is your target fund size and investment period?
questions.answer_text: Osprey Global Opportunities Fund III target: $3.0 billion. Current AUM: $2.7 billion.
Gross leverage target: 2.5x-3.5x. Net leverage range: 0.3x-0.8x long-biased. Maximum gross leverage: 4.0x (hard limit per fund documents).

85 employees total. Investment Team (38): Viktor Petrov -- Founder & CIO (founded 2011, 28 yrs experience), 5 Portfolio Managers (avg 16 yrs experience), 12 Senior Analysts, 10 Analysts, 10 Research Associates. Operations (28): COO, CCO, CFO, trading desk (8), middle office (10), legal (2), compliance (4). Client Service (12): Head of IR, 11 staff. Technology (7): CTO, 6 developers.

questions.question_text: How many total employees does the firm have?
questions.answer_text: 85 employees as of June 30, 2025.
Viktor Petrov: 60%. Osprey Holdings Ltd (BVI entity): 25%. Employee pool: 15%.

questions.question_text: Has there been any change in ownership in the last 5 years?
questions.answer_text: Yes. In 2022, the firm restructured ownership to include Osprey Holdings Ltd (a British Virgin Islands entity) as part of a tax planning initiative. Viktor Petrov is the sole beneficial owner of Osprey Holdings Ltd.

questions.question_text: Have any key investment professionals departed in the last 3 years?
questions.answer_text: Two portfolio managers departed in 2024: Jonathan Blake (Head of Credit, 8 years at firm) departed April 2024. Dr. Anya Volkov (Head of European Equities, 6 years) departed August 2024. Both positions have been filled.
$2.7 billion as of June 30, 2025.

questions.question_text: Has the firm been involved in any material litigation?
questions.answer_text: The firm settled a dispute with a former employee in 2023 regarding non-compete provisions. Settlement terms are confidential. No other material litigation.

questions.question_text: Provide gross and net returns for each fund since inception.
questions.answer_text: Opp. Fund I (2011): 14.2% gross / 10.1% net vs 5.8% HFRI FoF. Opp. Fund II (2016): 11.8% gross / 7.9% net vs 5.2% HFRI FoF. Opp. Fund III (2021): 9.6% gross / 5.8% net vs 4.1% HFRI FoF.

questions.question_text: Provide annualized returns net of all fees.
questions.answer_text: Fund III net returns: 1 Year 6.2% (HFRI FoF 5.1%, +1.1%), 3 Year 5.4% (4.3%, +1.1%), Since Inception 5.8% (4.1%, +1.7%).
Q1 2020: -19.4% (recovered in 11 months). 2022: -12.7% (recovered in 14 months). Both underperformed stated objective of capital preservation.

questions.question_text: Has the track record been audited?
questions.answer_text: Yes. Audited by Grant Thornton LLP.
No. The firm reports returns using internal methodology. GIPS compliance is under evaluation.
Management fee: 150 basis points on committed capital.

questions.question_text: Carried interest rate and preferred return?
questions.answer_text: Performance fee: 20% of net profits. No preferred return / hurdle rate.

questions.question_text: List all fees and expenses borne by the fund.
questions.answer_text: Fund expenses include management fee, performance fee, operating expenses, and transaction costs. Organizational expenses were borne by the fund up to $2M.

questions.question_text: What is the policy on transaction and monitoring fees?
questions.answer_text: Transaction fees may be charged on certain private transactions. Monitoring fees may be charged to portfolio companies. Fees are not offset against the management fee.
Estimated TER: approximately 350 basis points (inclusive of all fees and expenses).
Risk management is overseen by the CIO with support from a dedicated risk analyst. Daily P&L monitoring, weekly risk report, monthly stress testing.

questions.question_text: Is risk management independent from the investment team?
questions.answer_text: The risk analyst reports to the CIO. The COO reviews risk reports monthly.

questions.question_text: Any material risk limit breaches in past 5 years?
questions.answer_text: Two breaches in 2024: (1) Gross leverage exceeded 4.0x limit in March 2024 (reached 4.3x, corrected within 3 business days). (2) Single-name concentration exceeded 15% limit in August 2024 due to a merger arbitrage position (resolved upon deal closing).
SEC-registered. CRD Number: 295731.

questions.question_text: Any regulatory investigations or enforcement actions?
questions.answer_text: In 2021, the SEC issued a deficiency letter following a routine examination identifying inadequate disclosure of certain fee arrangements to fund investors. The firm remediated the identified issues. No formal enforcement action. In 2023, the firm paid a $450,000 civil penalty to the SEC to settle charges related to inadequate disclosure of conflicts of interest arising from the allocation of co-investment opportunities. The firm neither admitted nor denied the findings.
The CCO (appointed 2023, following the SEC settlement) reports to the COO. Annual compliance review. Quarterly testing program.

Conflicts of interest policy was enhanced in 2023 following the SEC settlement. The policy now includes detailed disclosure requirements for co-investment allocation, fee arrangements, and related-party transactions.
Grant Thornton LLP (since 2022). Previously audited by a regional firm (changed following the SEC examination).

One pending matter: A former LP has filed a demand for arbitration alleging breach of the LPA related to fee disclosures. The firm believes the claim is without merit. No reserve has been established.

questions.question_text: Amended ADV disclosures for disciplinary events?
questions.answer_text: Yes. ADV was amended in 2023 to disclose the SEC settlement.
No.
The firm does not have a formal sustainability policy. Sustainability factors may be considered on a case-by-case basis.
The firm follows industry standard cybersecurity practices. Annual security review conducted internally. MFA implemented in 2024.
No external penetration testing has been conducted. Internal security review is performed annually.

In 2023, a phishing attack compromised an employee email account. The incident was contained within 48 hours. No client data was exposed. This incident prompted the implementation of MFA across all systems.

gaps.question_text: What is your typical deal size range?
Describe your sourcing process and competitive advantages in deal origination.
What is your due diligence process for evaluating potential investments?
Describe your portfolio construction approach, including diversification targets.
What is your approach to value creation post-investment?
Describe your exit strategy and typical holding period.
Describe your investment committee process and approval requirements.
What is the maximum single investment as a percentage of the fund?
List all other funds or accounts managed.
Quarterly return history for most recent 5 years?
Total investments and realized vs unrealized split?
Does the management fee step down?
Code of ethics and personal trading?
Business continuity and disaster recovery plan?

flags.description: SEC enforcement action: $450,000 civil penalty paid in 2023 to settle charges of inadequate disclosure of conflicts of interest in co-investment allocation. Firm neither admitted nor denied findings.
SEC deficiency letter issued in 2021 following routine examination for inadequate disclosure of fee arrangements to fund investors. No formal enforcement, but indicates prior compliance weaknesses.

Fee structure: 150 bps management fee charged on committed capital (not NAV) combined with 20% carried interest and NO hurdle rate / preferred return. This is aggressive relative to industry norms for a fund of this size and strategy.

Total expense ratio of 350 bps is extremely high. For context, the all-in cost means LPs lose roughly 3.5% annually before the fund generates any net return. Net returns of 5.8% annualized imply gross returns are consumed substantially by fees.
Transaction and monitoring fees are not offset against the management fee, compounding the already high 150 bps management fee on committed capital. This creates additional fee layering concerns.

Risk management is not independent: the risk analyst reports directly to the CIO (Viktor Petrov), who is also the founder and majority owner. The COO only reviews risk reports monthly, providing limited independent oversight.

Two material risk limit breaches in 2024: (1) Gross leverage exceeded 4.0x hard limit, reaching 4.3x in March 2024. (2) Single-name concentration exceeded 15% limit in August 2024. Two breaches in a single year suggests systemic risk management weakness.

BVI ownership entity: Osprey Holdings Ltd (25% ownership) is a British Virgin Islands entity created in 2022 for 'tax planning.' Viktor Petrov is sole beneficial owner. Offshore structure raises transparency and governance concerns.

Two senior portfolio manager departures in 2024: Jonathan Blake (Head of Credit, 8 yrs) and Dr. Anya Volkov (Head of European Equities, 6 yrs). Losing two strategy heads in one year is a significant team stability concern, particularly given the SEC settlement in 2023.

Not GIPS-compliant despite managing $2.7 billion AUM. Returns are reported using internal methodology. For a fund of this size, lack of GIPS compliance raises questions about performance reporting rigor and comparability.

Cybersecurity incident in 2023 (phishing attack compromised employee email, contained in 48 hours) combined with no external penetration testing. MFA was only implemented in 2024, after the incident. Reactive rather than proactive security posture.

Pending LP arbitration: A former LP has filed a demand for arbitration alleging breach of the LPA related to fee disclosures. The firm has not established a reserve, but this echoes the same fee disclosure issues that led to the 2021 deficiency letter and 2023 SEC settlement -- a pattern of fee transparency concerns.

Changed auditor from a regional firm to Grant Thornton LLP in 2022 following the SEC examination. Auditor changes proximate to regulatory scrutiny warrant investigation into whether the prior auditor was dismissed or resigned.

CCO was appointed in 2023 following the SEC settlement, implying the prior CCO was inadequate or that the compliance function required significant remediation. The new CCO reports to the COO rather than directly to the board or managing partner.
No formal sustainability policy. Not a PRI signatory. For a $2.7B global multi-strategy fund, this may be a concern for LPs with ESG mandates.

follow_up_questions.question: Provide the full SEC settlement order from 2023. What specific conflicts of interest were inadequately disclosed, and what co-investment allocation practices were at issue?
What specific fee arrangements were cited in the 2021 SEC deficiency letter? Have all remediation items been fully resolved and verified?
Provide details on the pending LP arbitration: What are the specific fee disclosure allegations, what is the potential financial exposure, and why has no reserve been established?
Justify the 150 bps management fee on committed capital with no hurdle rate. Are there any fee breakpoints, most-favored-nation provisions, or fee reductions available for larger commitments?
Provide a detailed breakdown of the 350 bps TER. What are the individual components (management fee, performance fee, admin, trading, organizational costs)?
What were the root causes of the two risk limit breaches in 2024? What process changes have been implemented to prevent recurrence? Has the risk governance framework been enhanced?
Why does the risk analyst report to the CIO rather than the COO or an independent risk committee? What plans exist to strengthen risk management independence?
Explain the rationale for the BVI ownership entity (Osprey Holdings Ltd). What tax benefits does this structure provide, and how does it affect LP transparency and governance rights?

Provide details on the departures of Jonathan Blake and Dr. Anya Volkov. Were the departures related to the SEC settlement or internal disagreements? What is the experience level of their replacements?
Why did the firm change auditors from a regional firm to Grant Thornton following the SEC examination? Did the prior auditor resign, decline to stand for reappointment, or was the auditor dismissed?
What is the timeline for achieving GIPS compliance? At $2.7B AUM, why has the firm not yet adopted GIPS-compliant reporting?
Describe the new CCO's qualifications and the enhanced compliance program implemented after the 2023 SEC settlement. Does the CCO have a direct reporting line to an independent board or LPAC?
Has an external penetration test been conducted since the 2023 phishing incident? What cybersecurity enhancements beyond MFA have been implemented?
What is the GP commitment to Fund III? What percentage of the principals' net worth is co-invested alongside LPs?
Describe succession planning. If Viktor Petrov were to depart, what are the key person provisions and who would assume the CIO role?
Provide quarterly return history for Fund III since inception.
What is the distribution waterfall for Fund III? Is carry calculated on a whole-fund or deal-by-deal basis?
Describe the firm's valuation methodology for illiquid positions.
Does the firm have any plans to adopt a formal sustainability policy or become a PRI signatory?
Describe the firm's business continuity and disaster recovery plan.
